finding: §8.2 taper drops 'the lowest-yield non-always_on reviewer', but 'yield' is never defined (unique findings? findings surviving verification? findings only that role produced?) AND §9 dedup collapses each cluster to a `found_by` count of distinct roles — it discards which roles produced which findings. Per-role yield therefore cannot be computed from the merged output the taper is supposed to read. The two mechanisms are mutually incompatible as written.
suggestion: Define 'yield' precisely (e.g. count of surviving findings for which this role was the sole producer, i.e. found_by==1 attributed to it) and require the merge/record step to persist per-role finding attribution (role id -> finding count), not just found_by. Otherwise §8 cannot select a role to drop.

finding: Taper drops the 'lowest-yield non-always_on reviewer' but 'yield' is never defined. Is it fewest total findings, fewest unique (found_by=1) findings, or severity-weighted count? Tie-breaking when two reviewers have equal yield is also unspecified, and yield for a reviewer absent from the previous round (already tapered) is undefined. Two engineers will drop different roles, so the emitted panel is non-deterministic.
suggestion: Define yield with an exact formula (e.g. count of merged findings uniquely attributed to that role in the previous round), a deterministic tie-break (e.g. lowest yield then reverse-alphabetical id), and how a role that did not run last round is ranked.

finding: Clustering key is (location, class), but `class` is optional on findings (§16.5: add class only when the finding matches a mechanizable pattern). §9.5 says findings 'with distinct classes are never merged' — leaving the both-empty-class case undefined. Under a naive key, two genuinely different findings at the same location that both omit `class` collapse into one merged finding, silently dropping a distinct issue. This is a data-loss footgun for the common case (most findings have no class).
suggestion: State that an empty/absent class is NOT equal to another empty class for clustering — findings without a class are never merged with each other (only exact same non-empty class + same location merge). Add a test for two class-less co-located findings staying separate.

finding: §10.1 says roles_hash is 'stored in state.json next to spec_hash', but spec_hash is stored PER ROUND (ReviewRound.SpecHash), not once at top level. §10.2 requires roles_hash staleness to work 'exactly as editing the spec marks it stale', and StateStale compares the LAST round's spec_hash to current. For that to hold, roles_hash must also be per-round and StateStale/Converged must compare both. 'Next to spec_hash' is ambiguous between a top-level field and a per-round field, and the two designs behave differently.
suggestion: State explicitly that roles_hash is recorded per round (add ReviewRound.RolesHash) and that StateStale returns true if EITHER the spec_hash or the roles_hash of the last round differs from current. Update Converged accordingly.

finding: §11.1.3 introduces a 'spec-frontmatter role override' and §11.2 says an existing `tp: lens` block is 'auto-translated' to it, but the YAML shape of this new override is never defined. Is the frontmatter key still `lens`, or a new `roles`/`role_overrides` key? What is its structure (map of role-id -> {append lens[], or full role fields})? The v0.24.0 parser only knows `tp.domain` and `tp.lens` (keys implementer/tester/architect/all). Without the override schema an implementer cannot write the parser or the shim.
suggestion: Add a subsection giving the exact frontmatter override schema (key name, shape, whether it appends lens questions to a named role vs defines a spec-only role) and the mapping from each legacy `tp: lens` key to it — including the special `all` key.

finding: Clustering is by (location, class) but 'location' is free-form text (finding schema: 'section heading or line number'). Two roles citing the same place as '§8.2', 'line 84', and 'Adaptive Review Scaling' produce three distinct locations, so genuine duplicates are not merged and found_by/overlap ratio are under-counted. Because overlap ratio drives §8 taper, the taper decision is non-reproducible across equivalent inputs and untestable without a defined location-equality rule.
suggestion: Specify location normalization/equivalence (e.g. canonicalize to a section id, or match on normalized token) so that (location, class) clustering is deterministic and overlap ratio is reproducible.

finding: The convergence safeguard says a clean tapered round 'triggers one full-corpus confirming round before consecutive_clean can reach the required count', but never states whether a clean tapered round increments consecutive_clean (to 1) or is discarded. With review_clean_rounds=1 this is decisive: does a single clean tapered round converge (contradicting 'never declare convergence on a tapered panel') or force a full round first? Two testers write contradictory expected counters.
suggestion: State explicitly whether a clean tapered round increments consecutive_clean, and give a worked table for required=1 and required=2 showing which rounds are tapered vs full and the counter value after each.

finding: roles_hash is described as one hash over 'all applicable role files' content', but review and audit converge separately. It is unspecified whether roles_hash is a single combined hash (so editing an auditor marks REVIEW stale, and vice versa — over-invalidation) or per-phase. §10.3 reporting roles_stale on both --status commands does not disambiguate. A test 'edit an auditor, assert review not stale' has no defined expected result.
suggestion: Specify per-phase hashes (reviewers_hash / auditors_hash) or explicitly accept combined-hash over-invalidation, and define which phase each --status reflects.

finding: A spec-layer role override (§11.1 #3) lives in spec frontmatter, so editing it changes spec_hash, yet it is also part of the 'effective role corpus' that feeds roles_hash. It is unspecified whether editing a spec-local override bumps spec_hash, roles_hash, both (double staleness), or neither. Domain filtering also makes 'applicable' spec-dependent, so roles_hash varies per spec. The interaction is untestable without pinning which hash captures spec-layer overrides.
suggestion: State that spec-layer overrides feed spec_hash only (roles_hash covers repo-level .tp/ role files) — or explicitly define the combined behavior — and clarify that roles_hash is over the domain-applicable corpus for the given spec.

finding: An existing `tp: lens` block auto-translates to 'the spec-layer role override', but the old lens is unnamed/global while §11.1 #3 says an override 'appends lens questions to a named role (or defines a spec-only role)'. Which target the shim picks (append to every default role? a specific role? a new spec-only role?) is unspecified, so there is no defined expected output to test the migration against.
suggestion: Define the exact translation target: e.g. old lens becomes a spec-only reviewer role with a fixed generated id, or is appended to all active reviewers — pick one and give a before/after example.

finding: Overlap ratio = fraction of merged findings with found_by>=2. A round with zero findings gives 0/0 (undefined), yet a clean round is exactly when §8.2 compares the ratio to the taper threshold. Whether 0/0 counts as 'at or above threshold' (taper) or below (full panel) is unspecified and changes behavior in the most common convergence case.
suggestion: Pin the value of overlap ratio when the merged-finding count is zero (recommend defining it as 0, i.e. no taper signal, or route zero-finding rounds through the §8.4 full-corpus safeguard explicitly).

finding: Taper reads 'the previous round's overlap ratio' and 'lowest-yield reviewer' at emission time, but nothing states these are persisted. §10 stores hashes in state.json; §9 computes overlap only at --merge time. Without specifying that per-round overlap ratio and per-role yields are written to state.json (and read by the next `tp review`), the emission command has no data source and the taper cannot be tested end-to-end. Round-1 behavior (no previous round) is also not pinned.
suggestion: Specify where per-round overlap ratio and per-role yields are persisted and how the next emission reads them; define round-1 as full panel (no previous round -> no taper).

finding: Both the overlap ratio (§9.3, via found_by = count of distinct roles) and the taper decision (§8.2 'drops the lowest-yield reviewer') require every finding to carry the identity of the emitting role. But §3.3 fixes the finding contract without a role/author field, and §7.1 emits one prompt per role producing separate NDJSON. §9.1's `--merge` example (r1.ndjson r2.ndjson) reads like per-round files, not per-role. The spec never states that findings are tagged with their emitting role, nor whether merge input is per-role or per-round — yet found_by and per-role yield are load-bearing for §8 and §9.
suggestion: Specify that each emitted prompt/finding is tagged with its role id (an engine-injected field, not user-authored, preserving Principle 2's fixed contract), and define the merge input granularity (one NDJSON per role per round). State where per-role yield is derived so found_by and 'lowest-yield' are computable.

finding: The convergence safeguard says 'tp never declares convergence on a tapered panel' and requires a full-corpus confirming round, but whether a recorded round was tapered is not stored. Converged()/ConsecutiveClean() only look at each round's `clean` flag and the last round's spec_hash. Without a per-round 'panel was full vs tapered' marker, tp cannot exclude a clean tapered round from the consecutive-clean count, so the safeguard is unimplementable against the current convergence math.
suggestion: Add a per-round field (e.g. `full_panel` bool or `emitted_roles`/`panel_size`) to ReviewRound and change ConsecutiveClean/Converged so a clean tapered round does not advance the required count until a full-corpus round confirms it.

finding: §11.3 says the spec-layer override 'reuses the v0.24.0 resolve-at-read layering model (project defaults ⊕ local override)', but v0.24.0's local override is `.tp/local.json` (machine-local, git-ignored). The spec-layer override lives in the spec's committed frontmatter — a per-spec, committed, THIRD layer, not the project⊕local model. Claiming reuse conflates two different layers and leaves the precedence undefined (does frontmatter override the project role file, append-only, or replace lens?).
suggestion: Describe the role resolution as its own layering (project corpus ⊕ spec-frontmatter override) and state the merge semantics precisely (append lens vs replace, and what wins). Do not equate it with the config project⊕local model.

finding: §15.1 claims 'no role files means the embedded default corpus, identical to todays hardcoded behavior', but §8.5 sets `review_adaptive` default TRUE, which drops a reviewer in later rounds. Adaptive tapering is NOT identical to today's always-emit-3-roles behavior. On upgrade, an existing project with no config change silently gets round-2+ panels reduced — a behavior change contradicting the migration promise.
suggestion: Either default review_adaptive to false (opt-in, preserving identical behavior) and say so in §15.1, or amend §15.1 to acknowledge tapering as an intentional default behavior change and describe the impact.

finding: regression is a 'built-in always_on role, appended regardless of corpus', yet §16.4 implies every default role is ejectable as a worked example and always_on is a user-editable field (§3.2). If regression is ejected as a file and the user removes always_on:true (or deletes the file), does §8.3's 'never dropped' still hold? The spec both treats regression as intrinsically undroppable and as a data-driven field a user can change — contradictory, and 'eject then inspect reviewers/regression.json' has no defined expected result.
suggestion: State whether regression is ejectable; if hardcoded, exempt it from eject and from §5.4's 'no black box'; if a file, define behavior when a user edits/removes its always_on or deletes it.

finding: Principle 1 lists dedup as one shared code path ('share one schema, parser, validator, dedup, and staleness code path'), but §9 dedup by (location, class) is explicitly review-only: §9.1 scopes it to `tp review --merge`, and audit findings have no `class` field (§3.3 gives audit PASS/PARTIAL/FAIL + category taxonomy, not class). CLAUDE.md also records `tp audit --merge` as deferred/unimplemented. So dedup is NOT actually a shared review+audit code path — the 'separate data, share machinery' claim overstates the shared surface for its flagship example.
suggestion: Either (a) specify how audit findings dedup under the same code path (a shared key that degrades to (location, category) when class is absent), or (b) drop 'dedup' from the Principle 1 shared-path list and state that dedup is review-phase machinery, with audit merge remaining out of scope (consistent with §14.3 and the deferred audit --merge).

finding: The word 'lens' now carries three incompatible meanings: (1) the §11 'retired' `tp: lens` frontmatter, (2) the §3.2 role-file field `lens` (kept, first-class), (3) the §11.1.3 spec override that 'appends lens questions to a named role'. Meanwhile §11.2 says the `tp:` frontmatter 'is retained; only the standalone lens semantics are reframed' — 'retired' (§11 title/§11.1) directly contradicts 'retained/reframed' (§11.2). An agent told 'lens is retired' will be confused by lens fields everywhere. Additionally, the frontmatter KEY and schema for the new spec-layer role override (§11.1.3) are never specified — is it still `lens:`, or `roles:`?
suggestion: Pick one framing: 'reframed, not retired' if the frontmatter key persists. Rename the retired concept vs the kept field to remove the collision (e.g. keep role-file `lens` questions, call the frontmatter mechanism a 'spec-layer role override' and never reuse the bare word 'lens' for the retired block). Specify the exact override frontmatter key/schema (§11.1.3) so the shim in §11.2 has a defined target.

finding: `tp review <spec>` emits prompts and derives the round number from recorded state, but to taper it must read the PREVIOUS round's overlap ratio at emission time. Overlap ratio (§9.3) is produced by `tp review --merge`/dedup, and rounds are persisted via `tp review --record` into ReviewRound{round,findings,clean,recorded_at,file,spec_hash}. That struct has no overlap_ratio and no per-role data, so emission has nothing to base the taper on. The persistence path for the taper input is unspecified and the state schema does not hold it.
suggestion: Specify that `--record` computes and stores overlap_ratio (and per-role yield) on each ReviewRound, and that `tp review` emission reads round N-1's stored overlap_ratio. Add the fields to the state schema explicitly.

finding: §8.4 asserts 'tp never declares convergence on a tapered panel' and 'the clean round(s) that establish convergence run the full corpus', but §2.3/§14.1 state tp only EMITS prompts and never executes agents. tp cannot know whether the orchestrating agent actually ran every emitted prompt or attributed findings to every role — it only knows how many prompts it emitted. So 'the confirming round ran the full corpus' is not observable by tp and the safeguard cannot be enforced by the tool; it can only emit a full panel and trust the agent.
suggestion: Reframe the safeguard as an emission guarantee ('tp emits the full corpus for the confirming round') and make the convergence check depend only on data tp records (round clean flag + whether the emission for that round was full). State explicitly what tp can and cannot enforce given it does not execute agents.

finding: `found_by` is 'the count of distinct roles that independently surfaced' a cluster, but the fixed finding schema tp injects (severity, category, location, finding, suggestion, class) carries no role/producer field, and the current `tp review --merge` reads all input files into one flat list without tracking source. There is no specified channel by which role identity reaches the merge, so found_by cannot be computed as written.
suggestion: Specify the attribution source: either (a) merge counts distinct INPUT FILES contributing to a cluster and mandate one NDJSON file per role, or (b) add a `role` field the emitted contract stamps into each finding. Pick one and document it in §9.

finding: roles_hash hashes 'all applicable role files' content' with no defined ordering or normalization. If it concatenates files in directory-iteration order, the hash is non-deterministic across filesystems (false staleness on a teammate's machine). If it hashes raw bytes, reformatting a role file (whitespace/key reorder, no semantic change) marks convergence stale. Neither the input ordering nor byte-vs-normalized-JSON is pinned, so a determinism test has no fixed expected hash.
suggestion: Define a deterministic hash: sort files by id (or path), hash normalized/canonical JSON (not raw bytes), and state the algorithm — mirroring the v0.24.0 spec_hash rules.

finding: found_by is 'the count of distinct roles that independently surfaced' a cluster, but the finding NDJSON schema (role prompt) carries no role identifier. It is unspecified how merge attributes a finding to a role (by source file? by an injected field?) and what happens when one role emits two findings at the same (location, class) — counted once or twice. Without defined provenance, found_by (and thus overlap ratio and mechanize_candidates) cannot be computed deterministically or tested.
suggestion: Specify how role provenance is carried into --merge (e.g. one NDJSON file per role, or a required role field) and that found_by counts distinct roles, de-duplicating intra-role repeats.

finding: §3.2 exposes `always_on` as a user-settable role-schema field, illustrated only by 'e.g. regression' — but §5.2 makes regression a BUILT-IN role appended regardless of corpus, i.e. not a user-authored file. No stated need exists for a user to author their own always_on role. This is exactly the 'field just in case' that §14.5 ('No speculative role fields — new fields require a demonstrated need') and Principle 4 forbid. The one consumer of always_on is a hardcoded built-in, so the field earns no place in the user-facing schema.
suggestion: Remove `always_on` from the §3.2 user schema and handle regression's never-taper property internally (the built-in role is appended by the engine, not represented as a role file). If user-authored always_on roles are genuinely wanted, add a stated need and a non-regression example; otherwise the schema stays minimal per §14.5.

finding: §12.1 deletes `.tp-active` reading entirely; §15.3 claims 'no user action... v0.24.0 already migrated active pointers to .tp/local.json'. But v0.24.0 only migrated when the user ran `tp use`. A repo that had a `.tp-active` marker and never re-ran `tp use` under v0.24.0 will, on upgrade to v0.25.0, have its active pointer silently ignored and fall through to auto-detect — which errors when multiple *.tasks.json exist. The current code even hard-errors on a dangling .tp-active; that safety net is removed with no replacement warning.
suggestion: Specify a one-time migration/warning on v0.25.0: if a `.tp-active` file is present and `.tp/local.json` has no active pointer, either auto-migrate it or emit a clear warning telling the user to run `tp use <file>`. Do not silently drop the pointer.

finding: Hashing 'the effective role corpus (all applicable role files content)' is under-specified in two ways: (1) iteration/concatenation order is not fixed — filesystem read order is nondeterministic, so the hash must be over a canonically sorted set; (2) 'effective/applicable' is domain-filtered, so changing a spec's frontmatter domain changes roles_hash with no file edit, and a project with ZERO role files hashes the EMBEDDED defaults — meaning a tp upgrade that touches the embedded corpus marks every project's recorded convergence stale. Whether that global invalidation-on-upgrade is intended is not stated.
suggestion: Specify a canonical ordering (sort by directory then id) for the hash input, clarify whether the embedded default corpus contributes to roles_hash (and thus whether tp upgrades invalidate convergence), and clarify the domain-change interaction.

finding: §3.4 says 'tp lint validates role files' but the current `tp lint <spec.md>` takes a spec argument and does spec checks. The invocation that validates roles is unspecified: is it `tp lint` with no arg, a new `tp lint --roles`, or a side effect of every `tp lint <spec>`? Similarly §5.3 attaches `--eject-roles` to `tp init`, whose current form requires a `<spec.md>` arg — is the spec arg now optional for eject? Both command surfaces are ambiguous.
suggestion: Define the exact commands: how role-file validation is invoked standalone, and whether `tp init --eject-roles` requires/ignores the spec argument (or move eject to its own subcommand).

finding: The relationship between a role's `id` and its filename is unstated. `id` must be unique within its directory and kebab-case, but nothing requires `id` == basename. This makes `--eject-roles` overwrite detection ambiguous (§5.3 'refuses to overwrite existing role files unless --force'): if a user renamed a file but kept the default id, eject writes a new file with the default name, producing two files with the same id and a §3.4 lint error. Overwrite is presumably by filename, but collision is by id.
suggestion: Either require `id` to equal the filename stem (simplest, makes overwrite and uniqueness coincide) or explicitly define overwrite detection (by filename) vs uniqueness (by id) and the eject behavior when an id already exists under a different filename.

finding: The `regression` reviewer is a hardcoded built-in always_on role appended to every review, but collision handling with a user-authored `.tp/reviewers/regression.json` is undefined: does the user file override the built-in, get appended in addition (regression emitted twice), or trigger an error? §3.4's per-directory id uniqueness only covers on-disk files, not the built-in that is not on disk.
suggestion: Define precedence: reserve the id `regression` (reject a user role with that id, or let it override the built-in). State the rule in §5.2/§3.4.

finding: §6.2 says once role files exist, domain 'only filters roles via their domains field'. But the embedded corpus is partitioned by domain at the table level (§5.1), and ejected software roles carry no `domains` field (default = all domains per §3.2). So a project that ejects the software corpus and later writes a `domain: prose` spec would have all the software reviewers apply (domains absent = every domain). Whether eject stamps a `domains` field, or domain-partitioning survives eject, is unspecified.
suggestion: Specify whether --eject-roles writes a `domains` field on each ejected role (pinning it to the ejected domain), or clarify that post-eject domain no longer selects corpora and all present roles apply regardless of spec domain.

finding: §9.4 says found_by ≥ K 'extends the existing mechanize_candidates signal', but the existing signal (computeMechanizeCandidates) aggregates by CLASS across ROUNDS (rounds_seen>=2 or max-in-round>=5). found_by is a per-finding, within-round count of roles. How the two combine (fold found_by into the class stat, add a separate candidate list, OR-condition) is undefined, and they use different aggregation units.
suggestion: Specify the integration precisely: e.g. add found_by>=K as an additional OR-condition promoting a class to candidate within a round, and define how per-finding found_by rolls up to the per-class candidate list.

finding: `id` must be 'kebab-case' but no exact pattern is given. Cases like `role2`, `role-`, `-role`, `a--b`, `Role-A`, single-char, or digits-only are decided differently by different regexes, so lint boundary tests for id validity are non-deterministic across implementations.
suggestion: Give the exact validation regex (e.g. ^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$) and state whether leading digits are allowed.

finding: `--eject-roles` 'refuses to overwrite existing role files unless --force', but partial state is unspecified: if reviewers/ has files and auditors/ is empty, does eject write auditors and skip reviewers (partial), or refuse the whole operation? And does --force overwrite user-customized files wholesale (data loss)? A tester cannot assert per-file vs all-or-nothing outcomes.
suggestion: Define eject as per-file skip-if-exists (only writing absent files) vs all-or-nothing, and state exactly what --force overwrites.

finding: 'When a phase directory is absent or empty, tp uses its embedded default corpus.' 'Empty' is undefined: no *.json files, or literally no files (e.g. a stray README.md)? Also a directory with valid roles plus one malformed file is not 'empty' — §3.4 aborts with exit 3 rather than falling back to defaults, so a single bad file disables that phase entirely. The empty-vs-nonempty-but-invalid boundary is not pinned, so fallback-vs-abort tests have no fixed expectation.
suggestion: Define 'empty' as 'contains no *.json role files' and explicitly state that a malformed role file aborts (no default fallback), distinguishing it from the empty case.

finding: 'Lowest-yield' is undefined: yield could mean total findings, unique findings (found_by==1), or high-severity findings. Worse, yield is measured on the PREVIOUS round, but rounds improve monotonically (the author fixes findings between rounds), so a lens's yield naturally collapses once its issues are addressed. The heuristic therefore systematically drops whichever lens the author happened to fix FIRST, not the least-valuable lens — a genuinely overlapping pair can survive while a high-value, already-satisfied lens is tapered.
suggestion: Define 'yield' precisely (recommend unique-finding count, found_by==1, weighted by severity). Consider basing the taper on inter-role OVERLAP (drop the role whose findings are most fully covered by others) rather than raw yield, so already-satisfied-but-non-redundant lenses are not preferentially dropped.

finding: §8.4 says 'the clean round(s) that establish convergence run the full corpus' (plural) but also 'one full-corpus confirming round before consecutive_clean can reach the required count' (singular). With review_clean_rounds default 2, it is unclear whether BOTH required clean rounds must be full-corpus, or whether a tapered-clean round plus one full-corpus confirming round suffices. These give different convergence guarantees.
suggestion: State exactly which rounds must be full-corpus: recommend that every round counted toward consecutive_clean must be full-corpus (a tapered clean round does not increment the counter; it only triggers a full confirming round that may then start the count).

finding: Three load-bearing behaviors depend on unnamed internal constants with no stated default VALUES: the §8.2 overlap threshold, the §9.4 mechanization K, and the §8.2 'lowest-yield' tie-break rule. §13.2 keeps them internal (fine), but without indicative values an implementer must guess and no deterministic test for tapering can be written.
suggestion: Give the initial constant values in the spec (e.g. overlap threshold = 0.5, K = 3) even while keeping them non-configurable, and specify the tie-break for equal-yield roles (e.g. lexicographic by id) so behavior is deterministic and testable.

finding: §9.5 excludes findings with 'no location, or with distinct classes' from merging, but review's class field is optional. Two findings at the same location that BOTH omit class are not addressed: are null classes treated as equal (merged) or as un-clusterable (never merged)? This directly changes found_by counts and overlap ratio for the common case of unclassified findings.
suggestion: Define handling of missing/null class in (location, class) clustering — either treat absent-class as a distinct 'no-class' bucket that never merges, or normalize and merge same-location no-class findings; state which.

finding: The floor is 'one diversity reviewer plus always_on roles', but the taper's behavior when the effective corpus has exactly one diversity reviewer (e.g. a custom single-reviewer corpus, or prose after one taper) is not stated. Presumably taper is a no-op at the floor, but a tester cannot confirm the expected panel for the 1-reviewer case from the text.
suggestion: State explicitly that taper never fires (is a no-op) once the diversity panel is at the floor of one, regardless of overlap ratio.

finding: A spec-layer override 'appends lens questions to a named role'. If the named role does not exist in the resolved corpus (typo, or role filtered out by domain, or tapered), the behavior is undefined — error, silent ignore, or auto-create a spec-only role. A tester cannot assert an outcome for the dangling-reference case.
suggestion: Specify behavior when an override names a non-existent/filtered role (recommend a validation error with a repair hint, consistent with §3.4).

finding: §3.4 says 'an invalid role file aborts role-reading commands with exit 3' — a per-file check. But 'id unique within its directory' is a corpus-level invariant (two files, id collision) that requires reading all files, not one. Whether duplicate-id is enforced only by `tp lint` or also aborts read/emit commands, and which of the two colliding roles wins if not enforced, is unspecified.
suggestion: State that read/emit commands also detect cross-file duplicate ids within a directory and abort with exit 3, or define deterministic precedence if they proceed.

finding: `--eject-roles [--domain <name>]` accepts a domain, but embedded corpora exist only for `software` and `prose` (§5.1). §16.3 lists legal/product/data-schema/academic as guidance-only with no embedded corpus. `--eject-roles --domain legal` therefore has no defined outcome (error? empty dirs? fallback to software?), and the default when --domain is omitted (spec frontmatter domain? hardcoded software?) is unstated.
suggestion: Specify the default domain when --domain is omitted, and the behavior for a domain with no embedded corpus (recommend exit 2 with the list of supported domains).

finding: The always_on 'regression' role is central to §8 (never tapered) yet its lens is never defined. 'Regression' normally means 'did this change break existing behavior' — an implementation/code concern — but §5.2/§7.1 place it in the REVIEW (spec) phase, not audit, and §5.1 appends it to the PROSE domain too. What a regression lens checks on a spec document, or on prose, is unstated. A single hardcoded role applied across spec-review and prose without a definition is architecturally underspecified.
location: §5.2 / §8.3 regression role — undefined semantics and phase fit
suggestion: Define the regression role's lens explicitly in review context (e.g. 'does this spec revision contradict or silently drop a previously-guaranteed behavior/section?'), and justify or scope its application to prose. If it is code-oriented, reconsider whether it belongs in the review phase at all vs audit.

finding: §5.4 justifies eject as 'no black box' — making hidden persona prompts visible. But §5.2/§8.3 keep `regression` a hardcoded built-in, and §5.3 does not say whether `--eject-roles` writes a regression.json. If regression stays hardcoded and un-ejectable, its persona remains a black box, partially defeating §5.4's stated purpose; if it is ejected, §5.2's 'built-in always_on regardless of corpus' needs qualification.
suggestion: State explicitly whether --eject-roles writes a regression role file, and if not, note in §5.4 that the regression persona is intentionally not ejectable and why.

finding: §10.1 defines roles_hash as a hash of 'all applicable role files' content' — i.e. files only. But §15.2 asserts the staleness code paths 'run over the embedded corpus too,' implying the embedded default corpus is hashed even when no role files exist. These conflict: if only files are hashed, a project on defaults has a constant/empty roles_hash and a tp-version bump that edits embedded prompts would NOT mark convergence stale (silent divergence); if the embedded corpus IS hashed, then upgrading tp invalidates every project's recorded convergence — an unaddressed migration consequence. Neither branch is specified.
suggestion: State precisely what roles_hash covers: embedded corpus content (by tp version) plus any role files plus the domain selection. Then address the upgrade consequence explicitly (embedded-prompt changes invalidate convergence — is that intended, and is there a version-pinning escape?).

finding: The `tp: lens` shim must map legacy keys implementer/tester/architect/all onto corpus roles, but (1) if the project ejected/renamed roles, 'architect' may not exist as a role id, and (2) the `all` key means 'append to every role' — there is no defined translation of `all` into a single named-role override. Behavior when a referenced role id is absent (error, warn-and-drop, or create spec-only role) is also undefined.
suggestion: Define the shim's fallback when a legacy lens key has no matching role id, and specify how `all` translates (e.g. a special override that appends to every active role). Add tests for missing-role and `all` cases.

finding: `review_adaptive` is the first BOOL workflow field, and §13.1 says it 'inherits the v0.24.0 layering and validation'. But the resolver (configresolve.go) has only pickInt/pickString/pickChecks — there is no pickBool path, and parseWorkflowOverride/clampWorkflowRanges handle ints/strings/checks. The claim of inheritance glosses over new machinery that must be added (bool parse, layering pick, three-valued present/absent handling so 'unset' resolves to the default).
suggestion: State that a bool-resolution path (pickBool with pointer/absent semantics) must be added, and specify how an absent override resolves to the project default true. Note the parse validation (must be a JSON bool).

finding: 'Grounded in researched prompt-design best practices' and 'a web-research pass precedes authoring the embedded corpus and this guidance section' are process claims that cannot be verified from the delivered artifact — no pass/fail test can confirm research was performed or that guidance is 'grounded'. These read as unfalsifiable acceptance criteria.
suggestion: Reframe as testable deliverables (e.g. 'SKILL.md role-authoring section exists and covers items 1-5 of §16.2 with at least one worked example per §16.3 domain') and drop unverifiable process assertions from the normative acceptance surface.

finding: The taper for round N+1 depends on round N's overlap ratio and per-role yields, which must be persisted in state.json — §10.1 only mentions roles_hash there, nothing about persisting overlap/yield. More importantly, no rule resets the panel to full when the reviewed CONTENT changes: if the spec is edited between rounds (§10.2 marks it stale via spec_hash), the previous round's overlap was computed on now-obsolete content, yet §8 would still taper round N+1 on that stale signal — reviewing newly-changed spec text with a reduced panel. This is a false-convergence vector the §8.4 safeguard does not cover (it only guards the clean-round confirmation, not mid-stream spec edits).
location: §8 Adaptive Review Scaling — taper state persistence & reset
suggestion: Specify the persisted taper inputs. Add a rule: any staleness (spec_hash or roles_hash change) resets the emission to the full panel; taper may only accumulate across rounds where the reviewed content is unchanged.

finding: Adaptive tapering defaults ON, so every project reduces its review panel by default in its very first use of the feature — before any real-world data validates that the overlap heuristic (§8.2) and the §8.4 safeguard actually avoid missed findings. For an unproven heuristic whose only guard against false convergence is §8.4, defaulting to the aggressive behavior shifts the risk onto users who don't know to disable it. The AX token-savings argument is real, but so is the correctness cost of a first-release default.
suggestion: Consider defaulting review_adaptive to false for the initial release (opt-in), or explicitly justify default-true with the safeguard analysis and a note that the internal threshold is conservative. At minimum, document the risk trade-off in the spec so the default is a deliberate, reviewed decision.

finding: §3.3 makes 'a role file that attempts to declare output fields' a validation error, while §3.4 already rejects 'no unknown top-level keys'. It is unclear whether declaring an output field (e.g. `severity`, `class`) yields a distinct 'cannot redefine output contract' error or is just caught by the generic unknown-key check. A tester cannot assert which error message/behavior to expect.
suggestion: Clarify whether §3.3 is fully subsumed by §3.4's unknown-key rule (single error) or requires a distinct reserved-key error, and specify the expected message.

finding: review_adaptive 'inherits the v0.24.0 layering (project default, no task-file override needed)'. 'No override needed' is ambiguous between 'no override is required' and 'task-file overrides are not permitted'. Since v0.24.0 workflow fields are generally overridable per task file, a tester cannot tell whether a task-file review_adaptive=false is honored or ignored.
suggestion: State explicitly whether review_adaptive is overridable at the task-file layer or project-only.

finding: §16.4 mandates 'a web-research pass precedes authoring the embedded corpus and this guidance section'. This is an authoring-process instruction, not an implementable/testable requirement of the shipped tool, and cannot be verified from the artifact.
suggestion: Move the research-process note to a non-normative appendix or PR checklist; keep §16 requirements limited to what the shipped skill/corpus must contain.

finding: Phase is inferred from directory (.tp/reviewers vs .tp/auditors). A role file placed directly in .tp/ or in a typo'd directory (.tp/reviewer/ singular) has no defined handling — likely silently ignored, a hard-to-diagnose failure mode. A tester cannot assert whether a misplaced role errors or is ignored.
suggestion: State that only .tp/reviewers/*.json and .tp/auditors/*.json are read and that other locations are ignored (optionally a lint warning for likely-misplaced role files).

finding: Non-goals omit several boundaries an architect would expect stated, given the ruthless-minimalism directive: (a) no global/home-level role corpus — §4.2 anchors only at the repo .git boundary, so users expecting a ~/.tp shared corpus should be told it's out of scope; (b) no per-role or role-scoped convergence (convergence stays spec-level); (c) no role composition/inheritance/ordering (roles are flat and independent). Leaving these unstated invites scope-creep requests.
suggestion: Add non-goals for: global/user-home role corpus (repo-root only, deliberate for determinism per §4.2), per-role convergence, and role inheritance/composition.

finding: §11.1 says one of the three jobs of `tp: lens` was 'project-wide concerns' that should move to role files (§4) to be 'shared once instead of copied into every spec's frontmatter' (§11.1.2). But the §11.2 auto-migration translates every existing `tp: lens` into a SPEC-LAYER override (job 3, spec-local) — it cannot distinguish project-wide intent and so does not deliver the 'shared once' benefit automatically; users must manually promote overrides to role files. The spec claims the benefit without noting the shim leaves it unrealized.
suggestion: Note explicitly that the auto-shim preserves behavior only (spec-local override) and that consolidating duplicated project-wide lenses into role files is a manual follow-up, so the §11.1.2 benefit is opt-in, not automatic.

finding: The overlap threshold (§8.2) and K (§9.4) are 'internal constants' with no values, and the comparator for K (>= or >?) is unstated (only the threshold gives 'at or above'). Boundary tests (just-below vs at-threshold taper; found_by=K-1 vs K) cannot be written from the spec; they require reaching into unexported constants, and if those constants change, tests silently drift.
suggestion: Even if kept internal, document the constant values (or expose test hooks) and the exact comparators so boundary behavior is testable and change-detectable.

finding: §5.1 ships default corpora for exactly two domains (software, prose), but §16.3 mandates a documented 'short catalog of other domains (legal/contract, product/PRD, data-schema, academic) with their characteristic diverging lenses.' Documenting lenses for four domains tp provides no corpus for is speculative surface — mild tension with Principle 4 / §14.5's stance against machinery not earned by a stated need. It also creates a maintenance burden (a doc catalog that can drift from reality).
suggestion: Trim §16.3 to the domains tp actually ships (software, prose) as worked examples, and reduce the other-domains catalog to a single illustrative sentence rather than a maintained catalog, or gate it behind a stated need.
